steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The researchers hypothesize that PCFG whales' smaller size is 'an adaptation to distinct resource opportunities' in their foraging range. To directly support this, a finding must show both (1) what those resources are and (2) why smaller size would be advantageous for accessing them.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — When foraging along the coasts of Northern California and British Columbia, PCFG whales are in closer proximity to major ports and urban populations than ENP whales in the main group are when foraging in Arctic waters.: irrelevant to foraging resources or body size — ✗
  • Choice B — The average body size of PCFG whales observed along the coasts of Northern California and British Columbia has remained relatively steady in recent decades, while the average body size of ENP whales in the main group has slightly decreased.: discusses recent average-size trends without explaining any adaptive advantage for smaller maximum size — ✗
  • Choice C — When present along the coasts of Northern California and British Columbia, PCFG whales tend to forage in rocky kelp beds at shallow depths inaccessible to whales as large as those in the ENP main group.: directly shows that the PCFG foraging habitat requires smaller body size to access — ✓
  • Choice D — Certain crustacean prey species available along the coasts of Northern California and British Columbia where PCFG whales tend to forage are not available in the Arctic waters where ENP whales in the main group forage.: identifies different prey but does not explain why being smaller would be necessary to access it — ✗
C is correct.
